text:
🔥 Yameii Online: The Digital Dreamscape Queen 🔥 Welcome to the neon-lit, cyberspace utopia that is Yameii Online.

A Vocaloid rapper straight out of OseanWorld, she’s got the glitchcore hyperpop world on lock, bringing pixelated perfection to every track. With producer Deko at the helm, Yameii’s sound is a perfect storm of dreamy synths, rapid-fire bars, and futuristic vibes. This is what an AI popstar sounds like when the mainframe starts feeling emotions. 🤖💖 🌟

Tags That Define the Energy: Vocaloid? Check. Glitchcore? Absolutely. Hyperpop? You already know. Yameii Online is a genre-melting anomaly, blending hip-hop, experimental sound design, and synthetic euphoria into a sonic trip that feels like logging into a euphoric version of the internet. 🌐✨

🎧 Essential Tracks!: - Baby My Phone: A sugar-coated hyperpop anthem that sounds like a text notification overload in the best way possible. The beat bounces like a rubber ball, while Yameii's auto-tuned vocals give off major digital romance energy. listen here 🎶➡️ <https://open.spotify.com/track/2SjvBmZYIbQTJ7rrPgdmV8>

Yameii Vs. The World: Pure glitchy adrenaline. Feels like getting thrown into a high-speed anime battle with synth lasers flying past your ears. The energy is chaotic, unstoppable, and 100% iconic. listen here ⚡➡️ <https://open.spotify.com/track/2DcbKbERz9dTe4pYNqgbaC>

synesthesia!: The name says it all. This track paints colors in your mind with shimmering synths and a rhythm that moves like a dream sequence. Yameii’s voice is like a ghost in the machine, floating through a hazy soundscape. listen here 🌈➡️ <https://open.spotify.com/track/5vM8YrpSt5PH7usWXzJqgQ>

where’d u go? o(╥﹏╥)o .｡\*ﾟ+: A hyperpop heartbreak song disguised as a cute internet diary entry. The beat twinkles like fairy lights while Yameii pours out digital tears over an ex who vanished like an offline user. listen here 💔➡️ <https://open.spotify.com/track/5SPRSPKKKMYc1uMlu0fMgQ>

Floozie: A futuristic club banger with cybernetic swagger. Yameii’s delivery is smooth, the bass is deep, and the vibe is straight-up intergalactic. Perfect for a VR dance party. listen here 🕶️➡️ <https://open.spotify.com/track/2ZnBZWmkgLax15pVOclihg> 👥

Who’s In Her Orbit? Think of Yameii as the digital pop sister to artists like Dorian Electra, Fraxiom, and Alice Gas. She’s got that playful internet aesthetic with a touch of chaotic genius. If you’re deep into the hyperpop universe, you already know she’s a major player in the scene.

🌍💽 💎 Dive Into Her Universe: Yameii Online isn’t just an artist—she’s a concept. A fully realized virtual persona brought to life through sound, visuals, and lore. With Deko’s next-level production and Osean’s worldbuilding, she exists in a space where music, gaming, and digital art collide. Every song feels like a different level in a neon-colored RPG, where emotions are coded into the beat and every drop is a power-up. 🌐

Related Stars Shining Bright:

Deko: The mastermind behind Yameii’s sound, crafting futuristic beats that feel straight out of a sci-fi anime. 🚀

OseanWorld: The visual genius that gives Yameii her hyper-detailed aesthetic, making her an actual character in an evolving storyline. 🎨

ericdoa: Another hyperpop phenom mixing melodic flows with glitchy beats. 🔥

Ready to plug into the Yameii Online mainframe? One listen and you’ll be stuck in her world forever. 💾💫

🔥 OSNO1: The Glitchcore Phantom That Became a Hyperpop Queen 🔥

Alright, let's dive into the sonic chaos that is OSNO1, aka Laura Les, aka one-half of the legendary 100 gecs! This is where it all started before she became the nightcore vocal deity we know today. OSNO1 was a whole vibe—raw, experimental, and soaked in early hyperpop energy. This is the underground blueprint, the DNA of everything that came after. 🌀💿

🌟 Tags That Define the Energy:

Glitchcore? Check. Digicore? Yup. Electronic weirdness? Absolutely. OSNO1 was the moment before hyperpop exploded—screeching synths, distorted vocals, and lyrics that feel like an internet fever dream. Imagine if a Tumblr-era emo kid got thrown into a blender with a Vocaloid and a broken Game Boy. That’s the OSNO1 sound. 🎮⚡

🎧 Essential Tracks!:

\- A Conversation I Overheard: This one’s a full-on mind trip. The lyrics paint a surreal dinner table drama, laced with paranoia and obsession, all wrapped in a twisted electronic lullaby. It’s both eerie and hypnotic, pulling you into a glitchy dreamscape. listen here 🎵➡️ <https://open.spotify.com/track/7jUa6Loc6TGsmXqxpawkvK>

\- I'm Sorry: Pure emotional chaos. Layers of autotuned vocals, distorted beats, and unexpected sample flips create a rollercoaster of sound. This track feels like a hacked video game soundtrack with all the glitches turned up to 100. listen here 🎵➡️ <https://open.spotify.com/track/2WTf7B0kaG5F1recPgBfvX>

👥 Who’s In Her Orbit?

Before she was crafting chaotic bangers with Dylan Brady as 100 gecs, Laura Les was building her own universe of experimental electronic madness. OSNO1’s sound laid the foundation for what hyperpop would become. If you’re into artists like Fraxiom, Jane Remover, or Gupi, this is your sacred text. 🛸🔮

💎 Dive Into Her Universe:

The OSNO1 era was all about DIY digital punk energy. You can hear the early gecs DNA in every track—pitched-up vocals that sound like they’re screaming through a dial-up connection, beats that hit like an internet meltdown, and lyrics that make you feel like you’ve been sucked into a late-night forum post from 2013. This is the sound of hyperpop before it had a name. 🎶💥

🌐 Related Stars Shining Bright:

\- Dorian Electra: Gender-fluid hyperpop chaos wrapped in Baroque synths and medieval aesthetics. If OSNO1 made you feel something, Dorian will take you to another dimension. ⚔️

\- Food House: Fraxiom and Gupi’s unhinged hyperpop lovechild. Think broken synths, high-speed beats, and a refusal to take anything seriously. 🍔

\- Machine Girl: The noisier, more aggressive sibling of hyperpop. If OSNO1’s glitchcore vibes hit, Machine Girl’s breakcore assault is the next step. 💀

Ready to experience OSNO1 and witness the origins of a hyperpop legend? This is where it all began. Plug in, glitch out, and let the digital distortion take over. 🚀💿

🔥 Princess Ketamine: The Bubblegum Bass Baddie You Need 🔥

Okay, let's talk about Princess Ketamine. If hyperpop had a secret underground club, she’d be running the VIP list. This is glitchy, dreamy, and euphoric soundscaping at its finest, dripping in neon aesthetics and chaotic elegance. She takes bubblegum bass, twists it up, and serves it with a side of ethereal energy. If you love futuristic pop with a heavy dose of unpredictability, you’re gonna be obsessed.

🌟 Tags That Define the Energy:

Bubblegum bass? Check. Hyperpop? Hell yes. Ethereal electronic? All day. Her music feels like stepping into a fever dream where candy-colored synths melt into pulsating beats, and her vocals guide you through like a digital siren.

🎧 Essential Tracks!:

\- Weeds: This track is a poetic breakdown of emotion, wrapped in a dreamy instrumental that feels like floating in a glitchy, over-saturated world. Lyrically, it’s raw—touching on cycles of self-destruction and growth, making it both heavy and hypnotic. listen here 🎶💫 => <https://open.spotify.com/track/0UciSbxrzmIAelOirFJLKK>

\- Hikari: Imagine walking through a cyberpunk cityscape at night, neon lights bouncing off the raindrops. That’s the energy here. The track plays with dreamy, almost vaporwave aesthetics, layered with soft Japanese vocals and shimmering synths. listen here 🌸✨ => <https://open.spotify.com/track/2FA1QEFGkhIXXPj6OnoP53>

\- Down For Whatever - Princess Ketamine Remix: Absolute chaos in the best way. This remix flips the original into a hyperpop banger, with insane beat switches, rebellious lyrics, and enough attitude to make you wanna start a digital revolution. listen here 💥🖤 => <https://open.spotify.com/track/2guFO9dGgn6A2U2B91Lgc1>

👥 Who’s In Her Orbit?

If you’re vibing with Princess Ketamine, you need to check out That Kid, Fraxiom, and Alice Gas. These artists all bring different shades of hyperpop, blending elements of nightcore, electronic madness, and internet-era rebellion into their sound.

💎 Dive Into Her Universe:

Her music is the sonic equivalent of stepping into a glittering underground nightclub in the year 2087. She takes hyperpop’s maximalist tendencies and fuses them with something hauntingly personal—tracks like "Weeds" aren’t just bops, they’re emotional deep dives wrapped in auto-tuned beauty. Meanwhile, "Hikari" takes you into a dreamlike trance where synths sparkle like city lights.

🌐 Related Stars Shining Bright:

\- Alice Longyu Gao: Avant-garde, playful, and unapologetically chaotic. Think of her as the hyperpop high priestess. 🌈👾

\- Dorian Electra: The king/queen of genre-bending pop, blending electronic, glam rock, and club beats effortlessly. 👑💿

\- 100 gecs: The OGs of hyperpop absurdity, making music that feels like an internet glitch in the best way possible. 🤖⚡

Ready to fall into the digital dreamscape that is Princess Ketamine? Click play and prepare for a sugar rush of the future. 🚀💖

🔥 MACHINE GIRL: DIGITAL PUNK NIGHTMARE FUEL 🔥

If you’ve never had your eardrums obliterated by Machine Girl, you haven’t lived. This is cyberpunk breakcore at its most feral, a full-throttle sprint into the most chaotic, distorted, and glitchy soundscapes imaginable. Imagine a rave inside a malfunctioning arcade cabinet while a robot tries to strangle you—that’s the energy. 

🌟 PURE UNFILTERED SONIC VIOLENCE
Machine Girl’s sound is a lawless mashup of breakcore, punk, jungle, and noise, running at hyperspeed with distorted screams and frantic percussion. It’s the kind of music that makes you wanna punch a hole in reality.

🎧 Essential Tracks!:
- "NEON WHITE": Blistering breakbeats, insane vocal chops, and a sound that feels like you’re free-falling into digital hell. listen here 💥 => https://open.spotify.com/track/1U0IqWibsygyYcGm7EtL7h  

- "FULL METAL DISGUSTING": The perfect name for a track that sounds like an AI uprising set to the hardest drums you’ve ever heard. Unrelenting, raw, and dripping with cybernetic rage. listen here ⚡ => https://open.spotify.com/track/6HfOK6UxhXlQ6Q1sye6t2L  

- "THIS IS YOUR FACE ON DOGS": Glitchy, aggressive, and completely off the rails. The title alone tells you everything you need to know. listen here 🔪 => https://open.spotify.com/track/0AoI61bVJ4F3wRxlfHiI1F  

👥 WHO’S IN THEIR CYBERNETIC DEATH PIT?
Machine Girl is in the same universe as artists like Death Grips, Sewerslvt, and ProLaps—basically, if your music taste includes the words “chaotic,” “violent,” and “unhinged,” you’re in the right place.

💎 THE MACHINE GIRL EXPERIENCE:
It’s punk, but for the apocalypse. The BPMs are through the roof, the energy is feral, and the aesthetic is pure cyber-anarchy. Every track feels like a boss battle in a game that’s actively trying to kill you. It’s not just music, it’s an adrenaline rush straight to the bloodstream.

🌐 IF YOU LOVE THIS, DIVE INTO:
- Death Grips: The kings of digital insanity
- ProLaps: Hyperpop’s most deranged experiment
- Iglooghost: If Machine Girl made alien music

Turn the volume up, break your furniture, and let Machine Girl rewire your brain. This is the sound of the internet imploding. 🚨🔊💀

🔥 ProLaps: Hyperpop’s Unhinged Chaos Architects 🔥

Brace yourself because ProLaps is here to melt your brain and send you spiraling into the most chaotic rave of your life. This duo, made up of legendary internet provocateurs, blends distorted beats, ear-shattering bass, and unfiltered madness into a sound that feels like getting dropkicked into the future. If you thought hyperpop couldn’t get any wilder, ProLaps is here to prove you wrong. 

🌟 The Energy? PURE AUDIO ANARCHY
We're talking blown-out bass, over-processed vocals, and beats that hit like a brick to the skull. If you like your music aggressive, glitchy, and absolutely unhinged, ProLaps is the endgame.

🎧 Essential Tracks!:
- "CUMSHOT": Yes, they really named it that. This track is full-throttle destruction with industrial hyperpop energy that makes your heartbeat sync with a malfunctioning strobe light. listen here 💥 => https://open.spotify.com/track/3EjFbZxRjJGyVrxEX8hbZ2

- "HOT CUM": They are NOT subtle. A sonic assault that blends gabber, breakcore, and hyperpop into one of the most chaotic club bangers ever. Imagine a warehouse rave hosted by cyborgs on bath salts. listen here 🔥 => https://open.spotify.com/track/1GsCm7qpVmcq8vYw2HxMNo

- "LICKITY SPLIT": Fast, manic, and brain-melting. The BPM on this one feels like your body is being rewired in real time. listen here ⚡ => https://open.spotify.com/track/7pXjLb1n6aRzPjHQxF5TyG

👥 Who’s in Their Sonic Wasteland?:
Think Dorian Electra, Fraxiom, and the most insane corners of the hyperpop scene. They thrive in the digital underground, where sound is weaponized and nothing is sacred.

💎 The Vibes:
ProLaps isn’t here to make pretty music; they’re here to BURN IT ALL DOWN. This is club music for people who want to feel like they’re glitching out of reality. The production is aggressive, the lyrics are chaotic, and the energy is unmatched. You either love it or fear it.

🌐 If You Like This, You’ll LOVE:
- Machine Girl: Cyberpunk drum and bass meets unfiltered aggression
- Fraxiom: High-energy hyperpop chaos
- Lil Mariko: Screaming, distorted madness in pop form

If you’re ready for the absolute LIMIT of what hyperpop and club music can be, ProLaps is calling. Answer the call, crank the volume, and let the chaos consume you. 🚨🔥💽

🔥 Girls Rituals: A Sonic Wonderland of Weirdcore Vibes 🔥

Get ready to dive into the glitchy, indie-filled universe of Girls Rituals! This Canadian artist, aka Devi McCallion, has been shaking up the underground scene with an avant-garde sound that's both weirdcore and emotionally raw. With ties to projects like Black Dresses and an array of intriguing aliases (like Cats Millionaire and Hexi Wing), she’s the queen of reinvention and deep self-expression.

🌟 Tags That Define the Chaos:
Weirdcore, glitch pop, indietronica, and pure emotional artistry. Girls Rituals thrives in a space that feels both experimental and deeply personal. Her sound pulls you into a haze of layered synths and thought-provoking lyrics.

🎧 Essential Tracks:
\- "I Know": A wild ride of longing and chaotic energy! The lyrics scream rebellion ("I wanna prove what a stupid bitch I am") while wrapping it all in dreamy, glitchy beats. listen here 🎶
\=> <https://open.spotify.com/track/5Wbn03FclqhIk8ZUXwY7TX>

\- "Black Crow": Dark and poetic, this track captures existential vibes with lines like, "Every year that goes by, I just seem to get older." A deep cut for late-night reflections. listen here 🖤
\=> <https://open.spotify.com/track/53ZoauPxhSKSwjBLX6niS7>

\- "Treat Me Like a Doll": This one’s raw, heavy, and relatable AF. The chorus hits like a wave of self-doubt wrapped in a plea for connection: "Treat me like a doll, I'll be perfect." listen here 🪆
\=> <https://open.spotify.com/track/1K3u4GI2KIxWNU6ratzcKf>

\- "HRT": A poignant anthem about transformation and resilience. With a haunting outro, it feels like an emotional purge in musical form. listen here 💅
\=> <https://open.spotify.com/track/0Op9RhPgFoVhH0iSnD4fw7>

\- "Goddess": An empowering track with lines like, "Even when I'm feeling like a goddess, life isn’t perfect." It's a rallying cry to keep going, even when it sucks. listen here ✨
\=> <https://open.spotify.com/track/79UlQ8RJRktXoasMZYkBA4>

💎 Lyrics That Hit Different:
Girls Rituals’ lyrics are a blend of vulnerability and defiance. From self-exploration in "I Know" to existential musings in "Black Crow," every song feels like a page from a diary soaked in glitch pop magic.

💿 Dive Deeper:
Her albums, like \*Reddishness\* and \*Emergency!\*, are sonic journeys that deserve full attention. Each track builds on the last, creating an emotional mosaic that's impossible to ignore.

👥 Related Artists You Need:
For more vibes like Girls Rituals, check out her Black Dresses collaborator, Katie Dey, or glitchy legends like 8485 and Fraxiom. They’re all redefining what it means to feel through music.
Ready to spiral into Girls Rituals’ world? Trust me, it's chaotic, raw, and beautiful. Click play and get lost. 💻🎧✨

🔥 GFOTY: Hyperpop Royalty With Max Chaos Energy 🔥
Step aside, everyone! GFOTY, aka Girlfriend of the Year, is here, serving avant-garde pop chaos like nobody else. This British hyperpop queen blends bubblegum bass, experimental beats, and outrageous vibes that slay every time. 🎧✨
🌟 Tags That Define Her:
Escape room vibes? Check. Hyperpop innovation? Duh. This artist is in a genre league of her own with flavors of experimental madness and proto-hyperpop realness. 
🎧 Essential Tracks!:
- Friday Night: This track is pure weekend chaos! Lyrics like "Friday night, time to get drunk" embody the absolute insanity of a wild party anthem. It’s messy, cheeky, and unapologetically GFOTY. listen here (🍸✨) => https://open.spotify.com/track/1lgIlt243DVXg597hMx3Uf
- Stupid Horse (Remix): Featuring Count Baldor, this reimagining of 100 gecs’ classic delivers chaotic energy and wild experimental sounds. Perfect for fans of disruptive beats. listen here (🐴🔥) => https://open.spotify.com/track/0aV4YQ18bZ9qt0PDUZIDSz
- Poison: Dark, theatrical, and dripping in camp. The repetition of "Drink it all up, drink the f**king lot" makes it a hypnotic banger that screams hyperpop noir. listen here (💀🖤) => https://open.spotify.com/track/3Ruv9GEcztgqxDvbOL3ccT
- Tongue: This one's all about the vibe. The refrain "Deep inside, like a wet, wet slide" adds a quirky playfulness, wrapped in synth-driven beats. listen here (💋🎶) => https://open.spotify.com/track/38oHqKpGqxHkl6jR5CTqVT
💎 Dive Into Her Chaos:
With GFOTY, every song feels like a surreal party thrown in a candy-colored void. Whether she's shouting out ridiculous lyrics or delivering offbeat experimental sounds, her music always challenges what pop can be. Tracks like "Friday Night" highlight her tongue-in-cheek humor, while "Poison" dives into deeper, almost gothic tones. 
👥 Her Sonic Universe:
GFOTY shares her chaotic orbit with hyperpop legends like 100 gecs, Count Baldor, and A. G. Cook. She’s part of the iconic PC Music collective, known for reshaping the music world with bizarre yet genius beats.
🌐 Related Stars in Her Galaxy:
- Count Baldor: Bringing experimental beats to the next level. Perfect for GFOTY fans who love a chaotic banger. 💣
- 100 gecs: Icons of hyperpop insanity. Their energy matches GFOTY's vibe perfectly. 🌀
- Hannah Diamond: Another PC Music star, but with a softer, glittery aesthetic. 🌟
Ready to vibe with GFOTY and her wild hyperpop universe? Trust me, her chaotic energy will leave you spinning in the best way. 💿✨

🔥 Fraxiom: Hyperpop’s Glitchcore Genius 🔥
Fraxiom is the embodiment of chaos, creativity, and hyperpop brilliance! Originally hailing from Boston, Fraxiom’s music has been called "a mix of annoying and bad musical tropes but with a different vibe." Iconic, right? Their quirky, unconventional style makes them a true standout in the glitchcore scene. 🎶✨

🌟 Tags That Define the Energy:
Fraxiom’s music is all about hyperpop, **glitchcore**, and bubblegum bass vibes. It’s unpredictable, edgy, and full of surprises! Whether you’re a fan of experimental soundscapes or need some chaotic anthems, Fraxiom’s got you covered. 🌀💥

🎧 Essential Tracks!:
\- "8 now": This track is a wild ride through glitchy beats and clever production. It’s vibrant, weird, and totally addictive. listen here 🎶💥 => https\://open.spotify.com/track/3lzX0mU3pfXOQRnH0ryZJR

\- "mos thoser": Packed with sharp transitions and playful energy, this song feels like a kaleidoscope of emotions. Perfect for anyone who loves a high-energy soundscape. listen here 🎵🌈 => https\://open.spotify.com/track/6nEDYdxD1YEWnXdbWxT6U8

\- "kutna hora": A blend of eerie and upbeat tones, it’s a track that plays with contrasts and leaves you wanting more. The vocals carry a raw intensity that’s impossible to ignore. listen here 🖤💫 => https\://open.spotify.com/track/312w1yQem0YU0WBDn8RR4q

👥 Who’s In Their Orbit?
Fraxiom is one-half of the legendary duo Food House (alongside gupi), where they take their glitchcore aesthetics to the next level. They’re also linked to icons like Alice Longyu Gao and other experimental artists pushing hyperpop’s boundaries. 🌐✨

💎 Dive Into Their Universe:
Fraxiom doesn’t just make music—they create experiences. Tracks like "butterfly knife" are drenched in quirky creativity, while "scawy monstews and nice spwites :3" shows off their knack for blending humor with hard-hitting production. Every song is a rollercoaster ride, making you feel seen, heard, and hyped. 🔪🌀

🌐 Related Stars Shining Bright:

\- Food House: If you love Fraxiom’s solo work, you NEED to check out their collabs in Food House. Pure hyperpop chaos! 🚀
\- Alice Longyu Gao: The queen of absurdist pop who knows how to make every beat count. 🌈
\- Dorian Electra: Gender-bending anthems and experimental sounds for days. 🎭💎

Fraxiom is redefining what music can be. With their chaotic charm and undeniable talent, they’re paving the way for a new wave of experimental pop. Tune in and let the glitchy goodness take over your soul. 💿✨

Oh babe, Forget Basement is here to throw down a vibe that's colder than winter but still manages to bring all the heat 🔥! Here's what we got about them and their tracks—let's dive in! ✨

Forget Basement’s got this indie, alt-energy with sparse tags to box them in—classic underground vibes. Their bio on Last.fm is as mysterious as their sound, but hey, check out their artist page and their Spotify for the full drip:
listen here (add emojis) => <https://www.last.fm/music/Forget+Basement>
stream here, babes (all vibes, no skips) => <https://open.spotify.com/artist/1HTzkqGmbnKCyxPBeIjOBj>
Now let’s hit up some standout tracks from their discography, shall we? 😏

*   Wendy Carlos: Packed with energy and those witty, offbeat lines that make you do a double take (and then another). The bass hits like a truck, and there’s a raw edge in the lyricism, blending humor and existential musings. This is a self-empowerment anthem for the baddies who DGAF. listen here (wig snatch incoming!) => <https://open.spotify.com/track/3QckjbZ1wUsW6dsFSBW8K7>

*   To Be Washed Away: Ethereal, nostalgic, and poetic—this one makes you wanna stare out the window on a rainy day while feeling all the feelings 🥺. The lyrics dive into themes of loss and transformation, layered over a dreamy instrumental. If you’ve been thinking too much lately, this one’s for you. stream away (dreamy AF) => <https://open.spotify.com/track/2gGaBWjKtZaQiHx54evUw8>

*   LOU: Straight-up chaos energy, giving us chaotic good realness. It’s clever and self-aware, with that signature sharpness and humor you’d expect from Forget Basement. Put this on when you’re in your IDGAF era 💅. crank it here => <https://open.spotify.com/track/0qStCnFsxsmU0Viwdf1Gyu>
The crew’s low-key vibes and left-field lyricism give a fresh take on life’s chaos. Dive in and let the mood take over, babe! 💖✨

🔥 Food House: The Hyperpop Dream Team 🔥

Let’s talk about Food House, the duo that’s turning hyperpop into an absolute phenomenon. Gupi and Fraxiom, the geniuses behind this act, are blending glitchy beats, wild basslines, and vocals that’ll have you spinning in neon daydreams. Signed to Dylan Brady’s Dog Show Records, they’ve dropped their self-titled debut album, and honey, it’s a ride. 🚀✨

🌟 Tags That Define Their Vibe:

Hyperpop, bubblegum bass, electropop, and just a splash of hip hop. Think high-energy bops that’ll make your speakers cry, "Yes, more!"

🎧 Essential Tracks!:

\- "8 now": An intense hyperpop banger with layers of synths and intricate beats, perfect for diving headfirst into Food House’s chaotic soundscape. listen here (🔥) => <https://open.spotify.com/track/3lzX0mU3pfXOQRnH0ryZJR>

\- "mos thoser": Playful, glitchy, and packed with absurdist charm. This track is like a sonic amusement park ride. listen here (💥) => <https://open.spotify.com/track/6nEDYdxD1YEWnXdbWxT6U8>

\- "kutna hora": A moody masterpiece dripping with raw energy. Its shifting rhythms feel like a digital storm. listen here (⚡) => <https://open.spotify.com/track/312w1yQem0YU0WBDn8RR4q>

\- "Thos Moser": The track that started it all! A wild anthem showcasing their unique chemistry. listen here (🚀) => <https://open.spotify.com/track/6pe1T2thmWds9hPq67Xv74>

\- "ride": Dreamy, uplifting, and a total earworm. Imagine cruising through a pastel cyber city at midnight. listen here (✨) => <https://open.spotify.com/track/2pCBie4szXtgF3zVDFgnyw>

💎 Dive Into Their Universe:

Every track by Food House feels like stepping into a kaleidoscope of emotions, with sounds that are as experimental as they are catchy. Gupi’s futuristic beats collide with Fraxiom’s offbeat energy to create hyperpop anthems that are out of this world. Whether it’s the bold chaos of "butterfly knife" or the ethereal pulse of "sole," their music keeps your senses tingling. They’re here to rewrite the rules of pop, one glitch at a time. 🌈💽

👥 Who’s In Their Orbit?

Fraxiom and Gupi are surrounded by icons like 100 gecs, Alice Longyu Gao, and A. G. Cook, sharing stages and vibes with some of hyperpop’s most innovative artists. Their sound is part of a broader movement, and they’re taking us all to the future.

🌐 Related Stars Shining Bright:

\- 100 gecs: The architects of hyperpop chaos. Legends, period. 🌟

\- Fraxiom: Half of the duo and a solo star in their own right, mixing humor and heart. 🎭

\- Gupi: The production wizard making surrealist soundscapes. 🌀

Food House isn’t just a vibe; it’s a lifestyle. Get into their sound, and you’ll find yourself living in a pixelated utopia of music. 💿✨

🔥 Folie: The Glitch Queen of Hyperpop 🔥

Get ready to vibe, besties! Folie is serving experimental hyperpop realness straight out of NYC. They’re not just artists; they’re visionaries who use their platform to express complex identities in the most unapologetic way possible. It’s a reminder that being bold with your style can be just as powerful as the music itself.

Hyperpop is a community built on collaboration, where artists uplift one another and explore the boundaries of identity and sound. This genre is a melting pot of influences and styles that invites everyone to the party. Whether it’s through remixes or features, you see how this genre thrives on inclusivity and creativity. Hyperpop artists are redefining what it means to be an artist today, encouraging everyone to explore who they are, free from the confines of traditional labels.

At its core, hyperpop is a glorious celebration of self-expression, and it’s time to turn up the volume and embrace the chaos. It’s about owning your identity, celebrating differences, and creating a space where everyone can be seen and heard. The world is ready for more hyperpop, and it’s not just about the music; it’s about the message. This genre is breaking down barriers and inviting everyone to join the ride. 

So if you’re not vibing to hyperpop yet, what are you waiting for? Dive into this vibrant world and let the music guide you. Because trust me, hyperpop is here to stay, and it’s making waves that’ll shake the foundation of pop culture for years to come. 

Love ya, 
Camibabe 💖

PC Music is a record label and art collective founded by A. G. Cook in 2013, known for its experimental take on pop music. Emerging out of London, PC Music quickly became synonymous with a new wave of digital, hyper-stylized pop that blurred the lines between avant-garde music production and mainstream pop culture. The label’s sound is characterized by glossy, over-the-top production, pitched-up vocals, and a self-aware, almost ironic aesthetic that both celebrates and critiques commercial pop music.

A. G. Cook, the mastermind behind PC Music, originally studied graphic design, and his visual sensibilities heavily influenced the label’s branding and presentation. PC Music’s roster includes a range of eclectic artists such as Hannah Diamond, GFOTY, Danny L Harle, and QT, each bringing their own distinct take on the hyperpop sound. The label’s releases often come with high-concept visual components, contributing to its distinct identity.

The label’s initial releases, such as Hannah Diamond’s “Pink and Blue” and GFOTY’s “USA,” introduced a synthetic, surreal pop aesthetic that felt both jarring and familiar. These early tracks, characterized by their saccharine melodies and artificial textures, drew mixed reactions from critics but garnered a cult following for their novelty and audacity.

One of the major turning points for PC Music came with SOPHIE’s involvement. While not formally a part of the collective, SOPHIE’s collaborations with Cook and other PC Music artists blurred the lines between the two entities. Tracks like “Hey QT,” released in 2014 as a collaboration between SOPHIE and Cook, pushed the boundaries of what could be considered pop music. The song was ostensibly an advertisement for a fictional energy drink, playing into the hyper-commercialized aesthetic that defined the label’s ethos.

The label’s influence extended beyond music. In 2016, PC Music collaborated with Charli XCX on her *Vroom Vroom EP*, which became a pivotal moment in Charli’s transition to a more experimental sound. This partnership marked PC Music’s entrance into the mainstream and set the stage for future collaborations, including Charli’s critically acclaimed mixtapes *Number 1 Angel* and *Pop 2*.

PC Music’s impact on pop culture can be seen in its crossover with fashion and advertising. The label’s exaggerated, polished aesthetic has been compared to the visual languages of high fashion and digital media. PC Music artists have been involved in campaigns with brands like Nike and MAC Cosmetics, integrating their unique style into mainstream commercial contexts.

The label’s discography is a testament to its evolution. From the hyper-digital aesthetics of early singles to more sophisticated pop releases like A. G. Cook’s 2020 album *7G*, PC Music has continually pushed the boundaries of what pop music can be. In recent years, artists like Hannah Diamond and Danny L Harle have explored more refined, introspective takes on their earlier work, showcasing the label’s ability to adapt and grow.

PC Music’s legacy lies in its redefinition of pop music. What began as a niche, experimental label has influenced an entire generation of artists, giving rise to the hyperpop genre and challenging the boundaries between pop, art, and commerce. It has become a cultural touchstone, proving that pop music can be both deeply conceptual and accessible, blending high art with mass appeal.

The term hyperpop originated in the mid-2010s, shaped by the DIY ethos and experimental soundscapes of artists from the PC Music collective. AG Cook, the founder of PC Music, is credited with defining the early sound and aesthetic. Hyperpop blends elements of pop, electronic, and experimental music, characterized by its maximalist production style, high-pitched vocals, and eclectic mix of sounds and influences.

The genre gained traction through artists like SOPHIE and Charli XCX, whose collaborative works blurred the lines between mainstream pop and experimental electronic music. SOPHIE’s 2013 releases like Bipp and Lemonade established the genre’s unique sonic identity—mixing glossy, futuristic textures with raw, emotional undertones. Charli XCX’s Vroom Vroom EP (2016), produced by SOPHIE, brought hyperpop into wider recognition.

Hyperpop’s rise can be largely attributed to platforms like TikTok in the late 2010s. The genre’s catchy hooks, high-energy beats, and playful unpredictability made it ideal for short video content, where it found a new audience and rapidly spread through viral challenges and meme culture.

The genre’s visuals are as bold and diverse as its sound, often drawing from subcultures like rave, cyberpunk, and kawaii, while fashion trends tend to be androgynous and experimental. This blend of sonic and visual experimentation makes hyperpop a distinct genre resonating deeply with Gen Z, influencing both music and broader cultural trends.

Despite facing criticism for its heavy reliance on autotune and the perceived appropriation of elements from other genres like hip-hop and RnB, hyperpop has continued to evolve, pushing boundaries and redefining modern pop music. Its impact has been so profound that it’s become a key influence on mainstream pop, proving that its reach extends far beyond its niche beginnings.

Bladee, born Benjamin Reichwald on April 9, 1994, in Stockholm, Sweden, is a multidimensional artist known for his genre-bending music and visual art. He started his journey in music at a young age, heavily influenced by the internet’s vast underground music scene. Bladee co-founded Drain Gang (previously known as Gravity Boys) along with Ecco2k and Thaiboy Digital, establishing a unique sound that blends lo-fi cloud rap with experimental pop and ambient influences.

One of the most intriguing aspects of Bladee’s career is his holistic approach to artistry. He prefers working with one producer per album to ensure consistency in sound, often collaborating with Whitearmor and Gud, two of his long-time musical partners. Bladee’s discography is an ever-evolving saga, featuring albums like Eversince, Red Light, 333, and Spiderr. Each project showcases a new chapter in his creative vision, filled with surreal lyrics, heavily autotuned vocals, and complex emotional themes.

Bladee’s connection with spirituality is central to his identity. He openly discusses his belief in God and how it influences his work. His music is often introspective, exploring themes of existentialism, spirituality, and identity. In an interview, he shared that he views the material world as “trash” and values the internal world more, describing the world as a “trash island” and stating that true power comes from creating one’s own reality. This philosophy underpins much of his music and visual art, which often appears abstract and surreal.

Bladee’s real-life experiences also feed into his mythos. During a trip to Thailand, he claims to have been struck by lightning, a moment that significantly impacted his perspective on life and creativity. Despite the incident, he avoided seeking medical attention and described the experience as elevating him to a new level of understanding and seeing the world in a different light.

His fashion and visual art are as distinctive as his music. Bladee has worked on multiple visual projects, including cover art for his albums and fashion collaborations. He recently partnered with GANT to create a collection that mixes classic preppy styles with his signature Drain Gang aesthetic. The collection, named Drain Gant, represents a fusion of traditional clothing styles and Bladee’s own subcultural influence, highlighting the contrast between formal and alternative fashion.

In recent years, Bladee’s music has grown more uplifting and melodic, diverging from the darker, more melancholic tones of his early works. His loyal fanbase, known as “Drainers,” has been following his journey closely, analyzing his cryptic lyrics, fashion choices, and artistic projects. Fans are often deeply engaged with the enigmatic and sometimes ambiguous content he shares, with many even attributing spiritual or supernatural elements to his persona.

Bladee’s creative process is deeply personal, and he sees his output as more than just music—it’s a means of creating a world within which he exists. His unique style and philosophical approach have made him a prominent figure in the experimental music scene, and he continues to inspire a new generation of artists with his boundary-pushing work.

Qlimax, one of the premier hardstyle events in the world, was launched by Q-dance in 2000. Over the years, it has become a cornerstone event for the harder styles of electronic music, featuring some of the most innovative stage designs, anthems, and audiovisual storytelling experiences. Initially, Qlimax was not exclusively focused on hardstyle. Early lineups included artists from hard trance and hard house genres, such as Armin van Buuren and Ferry Corsten, but the festival gradually shifted its focus to hardstyle and hardcore music by 2003.

Held annually at the GelreDome in Arnhem, Netherlands, Qlimax has developed a reputation for its theatrical themes, intricate stage designs, and unforgettable visual effects. Each edition revolves around a central theme, with top-tier artists creating exclusive anthems that capture the essence of that year's storyline. DJs like Headhunterz, Technoboy, and The Prophet have been regular fixtures at Qlimax, with Technoboy holding the record for the most performances.

Qlimax’s history has also included several international editions, with events in Belgium in 2006 and 2007. The brand has experimented with multiple editions per year, but since 2004, it has focused on a single, highly-anticipated annual edition. Despite interruptions caused by the COVID-19 pandemic, Qlimax adapted by hosting virtual events, showcasing its dedication to the community.

In 2024, Q-dance announced that Qlimax would be hosting its final edition titled "The Final Prophecy," marking the end of an era for the festival. This decision comes as part of a broader shift within Q-dance’s event strategy, following the cancellation of other major events like Qapital and X-Qlusive.

Dekmantel Festival, founded in 2013, emerged as a result of a small collective’s deep passion for electronic music. The origins of Dekmantel trace back to the mid-2000s, when founders Thomas Martojo, Casper Tielrooij, and Jan van Kampen began hosting intimate parties in The Hague and Amsterdam. Their love for Detroit techno and early American house music led them to start their own events, which featured acts like San Proper and Robert Hood in small venues. This underground success laid the groundwork for launching Dekmantel as a festival.

The transition from club nights to a full-scale festival began with the establishment of the Dekmantel label in 2009. The label’s first releases included works by Juju & Jordash and San Proper, setting a high standard for quality and originality. As the label grew, so did the ambitions of the founders, and in 2013, they hosted the inaugural Dekmantel Festival in the Amsterdamse Bos, a lush park just outside Amsterdam. With a carefully curated lineup that included John Talabot, Levon Vincent, and Move D, the festival’s debut edition was a huge success and set the tone for future editions.

One of Dekmantel’s defining characteristics is its emphasis on artistic integrity and musical diversity. From its inception, the festival aimed to create a balanced lineup that highlights both established artists and emerging talent. Dekmantel also places a strong emphasis on maintaining an intimate atmosphere, with thoughtfully designed stages like the Selectors Stage and the UFO Stage, each offering a unique vibe and experience. The Selectors Stage, set under a majestic tree, is known for its eclectic daytime sets, while the UFO Stage explores the more experimental and industrial side of electronic music.

The festival’s success in Amsterdam led to further expansions. In 2016, Dekmantel Selectors was launched in Tisno, Croatia, offering a more intimate experience focused on leftfield and obscure electronic music. Other spin-off events include Lente Kabinet, a smaller festival held in Amsterdam, and the Dekmantel Selectors stages featured at festivals around the world. Dekmantel’s global influence also extends to collaborative events and special showcases in cities like São Paulo and Bogotá, connecting their Amsterdam roots with international audiences.

Dekmantel’s reputation for high-quality production and innovative programming has solidified its place as one of the most respected electronic music festivals in the world. Over the years, it has hosted a who’s who of electronic music, including techno pioneers like Jeff Mills and Nina Kraviz, as well as experimental and avant-garde acts such as Floating Points and Four Tet. The festival’s decade-long success is attributed not just to its ability to curate incredible lineups, but also to its strong community ethos and commitment to maintaining the intimate, underground feel that characterized its early years.

In addition to the main festival, Dekmantel has continued to push boundaries with unique collaborations and ambitious programming. For example, in 2019, the Radar Stage was introduced, replacing the former Boiler Room stage. This new stage format allowed for greater artistic control and became a highlight for the festival, showcasing experimental artists and more unconventional performances. Dekmantel’s ability to adapt and innovate each year ensures its continued relevance and influence within the global electronic music scene.

Today, Dekmantel Festival is not only a highlight of the European festival calendar, but also a benchmark for quality, creativity, and community in electronic music. As the festival continues to evolve, it remains true to its original spirit, offering a space for artists and fans alike to connect over a shared love of cutting-edge music and culture.

Electric Daisy Carnival, known as EDC, kicked off back in 1997 as a raw, underground warehouse party in Los Angeles. Pasquale Rotella, the mastermind behind Insomniac, wanted to create something that felt like a crossover between a rave and a carnival. EDC quickly outgrew its early days and became a hub for electronic music enthusiasts, bringing a mix of carnival rides, art installations, and performances that made it stand out from the rest.

The festival kept getting bigger each year. By 2008, EDC was pulling crowds so large that it moved to the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum and started popping up in other cities like Colorado and Puerto Rico. But 2010 was a turning point when a tragic incident at EDC Los Angeles resulted in the death of a 15-year-old. The fallout from this incident led to EDC being banned from Los Angeles venues, forcing Insomniac to rethink its strategy.

In 2011, EDC found a new home at the Las Vegas Motor Speedway, transforming the festival into something massive and unforgettable. With three days of non-stop music, over 230,000 people flooded the speedway, making it the biggest electronic music event in North America. This move didn’t just make EDC bigger—it made it legendary, turning the festival into a must-attend destination for fans around the world. The stages became larger-than-life productions, and the overall experience became more immersive, creating a full-blown spectacle of lights, sound, and jaw-dropping visuals.

By 2013, EDC was going global, debuting in places like London, São Paulo, and Mexico City. EDC Mexico quickly became a fan favorite, drawing massive crowds every year since 2014. The Las Vegas edition continued to up its game, with more stages, bigger acts, and new features like Camp EDC in 2018, which gave fans a chance to stay on-site and be part of the festival’s unique energy all weekend long.

In 2013, a major shift happened when Live Nation acquired a 50% stake in Insomniac for a reported $50 million. SFX Entertainment had also been in the race, allegedly offering up to $100 million, but the deal went to Live Nation. This partnership gave Insomniac the resources to elevate its festivals even further. With Live Nation’s backing, EDC started expanding into markets like Japan, Korea, and China, securing its place as a top-tier global festival brand. The added infrastructure, increased safety protocols, and expanded production capabilities brought the festival to new heights, all while Pasquale Rotella remained at the creative helm, ensuring EDC stayed true to its roots.

EDC has always been about pushing boundaries. Over the years, it has hosted iconic sets from industry giants like Tiësto, Armin van Buuren, Kaskade, and Carl Cox, as well as surprise appearances and unforgettable stage moments. Stages like the “Kinetic Field” and “Cosmic Meadow” have become almost mythical in the EDM community for their sheer scale and intricate designs.

Beyond the music, EDC has become an economic powerhouse for Las Vegas, pumping millions into the local economy every year. It’s more than just a weekend—it’s a whole week of events. EDC Week, a series of exclusive nightclub and pool parties, turns Las Vegas into the ultimate party playground in the days leading up to the festival. 

EDC faced its share of hurdles, from controversies in Los Angeles to challenges posed by the COVID-19 pandemic. But it kept evolving, embracing new markets and ideas. In 2024, Insomniac introduced EDSea, a cruise festival experience combining the EDC vibe with a high-seas adventure, showing that the brand is always exploring new ways to connect with its fans.

Today, EDC is a cultural juggernaut. It’s not just a festival; it’s a community built on the values of unity, love, creativity, and self-expression. The partnership with Live Nation has been instrumental in scaling EDC to a global stage while keeping its core essence intact. With every new edition, EDC continues to redefine what a festival experience can be, setting the standard for festivals around the world.

MUTEK was founded in 2000 by Alain Mongeau in Montreal, Canada, with the goal of creating a platform that showcased cutting-edge electronic music and digital arts. The festival began as a small, innovative event focusing on live performances, experimental electronic sounds, and digital creativity. From the very beginning, MUTEK differentiated itself from other electronic music festivals by emphasizing live sets and collaborative artistic performances rather than DJ sets. The name MUTEK is a combination of "mutation" and "technology," reflecting its mission to evolve and adapt within the ever-changing landscape of digital arts and music.

The first edition of MUTEK in 2000 featured a modest lineup of around 30 artists, including Canadian talents like Tim Hecker and Akufen, who have since gained international recognition. The festival's programming included performances, panels, and discussions that aimed to foster dialogue and exchange between artists, industry professionals, and audiences. MUTEK quickly became a space for experimentation and exploration, encouraging collaborations between musicians and visual artists. This unique approach solidified its position as a leading platform for avant-garde electronic music and digital art in North America.

After several successful editions in Montreal, MUTEK began to expand its reach internationally, starting with Latin America. MUTEK launched its first international edition in Argentina in 2002, followed by Chile in 2003 and Mexico in 2004. These Latin American editions provided new opportunities for local and international artists to connect and collaborate, building a strong community of electronic music and digital art enthusiasts across the region. MUTEK Mexico, in particular, became a pivotal event for electronic music and digital arts in Latin America, celebrating its 20th anniversary in 2024.

The expansion continued with editions in cities like Barcelona in 2009, Tokyo in 2016, and Dubai in 2017, as well as smaller experimental "Micro MUTEK" events in various cities around the world. Each international edition of MUTEK retains the core values and mission of the original Montreal festival but adapts to the unique cultural and artistic contexts of its host city. This global network has helped MUTEK become a significant cultural phenomenon, fostering a sense of unity and shared creativity among artists and audiences worldwide.

Throughout its history, MUTEK has remained committed to its original mission of promoting live performance and innovation in electronic music and digital art. In 2014, MUTEK partnered with Montreal's Elektra festival to create the EM15 event, further blending music with digital arts and expanding its scope. This partnership highlighted MUTEK’s ongoing dedication to exploring new formats and evolving its programming.

MUTEK has also played a crucial role in the careers of many Canadian and international artists. Local Montreal artists like Akufen, Scott Monteith, and Herman Kolgen have used MUTEK as a launchpad to gain wider recognition. The festival’s influence extends beyond North America, providing a platform for artists from around the world to present their work in new and dynamic contexts.

Despite facing challenges such as the economic downturn in the mid-2000s and logistical difficulties with international expansions, MUTEK has managed to sustain its vision and growth. The festival’s resilience and adaptability have allowed it to maintain its relevance and continue to push boundaries in electronic music and digital arts. The organization has embraced new technologies and formats, including virtual editions during the COVID-19 pandemic, ensuring that the spirit of MUTEK remains strong even in difficult times.

MUTEK’s legacy is one of fostering creativity, innovation, and community. Its emphasis on live performance and experimental programming has made it a key player in the evolution of electronic music and digital arts. Today, MUTEK remains one of the most respected and influential festivals of its kind, continuing to inspire and shape the global landscape of electronic music and digital creativity.

Thunderdome is a legendary Dutch music festival and brand dedicated to hardcore and gabber music, and it has been an integral part of the electronic dance music scene since its inception in 1992. Created by ID\&T, the same organization behind events like Mysteryland and Sensation, Thunderdome emerged as a cultural movement that defined the harder styles of dance music.

The very first Thunderdome event was held on October 3, 1992, at the Thialf ice rink in Heerenveen, the Netherlands. It featured a massive octopus fairground ride, and the event's underground success set the stage for what would become one of the most iconic hardcore music festivals in the world. This early edition was just the second major event organized by ID\&T after The Final Exam, a hardcore rave that marked the beginning of the brand's foray into large-scale events. Thunderdome's raw and unfiltered approach to music and culture became a blueprint for hardcore events globally.

Thunderdome quickly grew in popularity throughout the 1990s, establishing itself as the leading force in the hardcore scene. The festival's CD compilations, released in collaboration with Arcade Records and later through ID\&T’s own label, became synonymous with the sound of the genre, helping to spread the music worldwide. By 1995, the Thunderdome brand had sold over 1.5 million compilation albums, making it a cultural phenomenon that reached far beyond the confines of the Netherlands.

The festival's history is marked by several iconic editions and venues, such as the Thunderdome Decade edition in 2002 at the Amsterdam RAI, which celebrated ten years of the brand, and the 2007 edition, held at the Heineken Music Hall, titled Thunderdome XV. Each year brought a unique theme, elaborate stage designs, and legendary DJ performances from artists like Angerfist, DJ Dano, and Buzz Fuzz, who became staples of the Thunderdome experience.

After 20 years of continuous success, the festival held its so-called Final Exam edition in 2012, which was marketed as the last Thunderdome event ever. However, due to overwhelming demand and the enduring legacy of the brand, Thunderdome made a massive comeback in 2017 with its 25th-anniversary edition at the Jaarbeurs in Utrecht. This event was a spectacular return, breaking records with 50,000 attendees and reinforcing its status as the premier hardcore event in the world.

Thunderdome's impact is not limited to its events alone. It has a dedicated fanbase and an entire subculture surrounding it, complete with its own fashion styles, artwork, and a distinctive community spirit. In 2019, a documentary titled Thunderdome Never Dies was released, chronicling the festival’s rich history and cultural significance. The documentary delves into the rise of gabber culture and Thunderdome’s influence on the global hardcore music scene.

Today, Thunderdome continues to push the boundaries of hardcore music, hosting new editions and expanding its presence globally. The festival remains true to its roots, celebrating the hardcore and gabber sound with unmatched energy and a dedication to preserving the history and future of one of dance music’s most intense and beloved genres.

Mysteryland, founded in 1993 by ID&T, holds the title of the world's longest-running electronic music festival. It was the first of its kind in the Netherlands, originally starting as a one-day event that focused on bringing together various genres of electronic music under one banner. The festival’s early editions were characterized by their underground vibe, featuring house, trance, and techno artists who were pioneers of the dance music scene.

In its initial years, Mysteryland changed locations frequently, with early editions held in cities like Rotterdam and Utrecht before finding its permanent home at the Floriade terrain in Haarlemmermeer in 2003. This location allowed for the event to expand its scale and offerings, solidifying its reputation as a premier festival. By 2002, Mysteryland was operating as a one-day event and continued in this format until 2013, when it expanded to a multi-day experience, reflecting its growing popularity and demand.

Each year, Mysteryland brings together over 300 artists performing on more than 20 stages, covering a wide array of genres from house, techno, and trance to hardstyle, drum and bass, and even hip-hop and R&B. The festival is renowned for its creative and eclectic stage designs, with each stage offering a unique visual and auditory experience. Attendees also have the chance to explore intimate, hidden stages scattered across the grounds, creating a sense of discovery and immersion.

The festival’s camping experience, known for its dedicated "Party Animals," adds another dimension to Mysteryland’s unique atmosphere. Campers are treated to exclusive pre- and after-parties, wellness areas, food markets, and even creative spaces where attendees can get tattoos or style their outfits. This sense of community and the shared experience has helped build a loyal following that returns year after year.

Mysteryland’s influence extends beyond the Netherlands, as it has hosted international editions in countries like Chile and the United States. The festival’s first U.S. edition in 2014 was held at the legendary grounds of the Woodstock Festival in Bethel Woods, New York, bridging the gap between dance music and one of the most iconic locations in music history.

Over the years, Mysteryland has seen performances from world-renowned artists such as Paul Oakenfold, Armin van Buuren, Carl Cox, David Guetta, and Tiësto. Its reputation for offering a diverse lineup has made it a staple in the global festival circuit, and its motto, “Yesterday is history, today is a gift, tomorrow is a mystery,” encapsulates the ethos of the festival—celebrating the present moment and the ever-evolving landscape of electronic music.

Today, Mysteryland continues to push boundaries, introducing new themes and creative concepts each year. With its stunning end-shows featuring fireworks, light displays, and synchronized music, it leaves an indelible mark on all attendees and stands as a testament to the power of electronic music to unite people from around the world.

Sensation Black was introduced in 2002 as a counterpart to Sensation White, catering to fans of harder electronic dance music genres. Created by the Dutch event company ID\&T, Sensation Black focused on genres like hard trance, hardstyle, and hardcore, offering a darker and more intense experience compared to the uplifting and melodic atmosphere of Sensation White.

The event was held annually at the Amsterdam ArenA (now Johan Cruijff ArenA), typically in the second weekend of July, and required all attendees to dress in black, creating a visually striking contrast to the all-white dress code of its sister event.

Sensation Black featured some of the most prominent names in harder styles of electronic music, including Showtek, Angerfist, Headhunterz, DJ Lady Dana, DJ Luna, and many others. Its high-energy atmosphere, combined with state-of-the-art stage designs, pyrotechnics, and spectacular visual effects, made it one of the most popular hard dance music events in the world. Sensation Black also distinguished itself with thematic productions and meticulously curated DJ lineups, establishing a strong following among fans of harder dance music genres.

One of the more controversial moments in Sensation Black’s history occurred in 2006 when DJ Rob Gee and his live band Ampt performed a set that incorporated more rock and metal elements, which was not well-received by the audience. The performance led to a negative reaction from the crowd, resulting in disruptions and a heated altercation on stage, which led to the cancellation of the remainder of the set. This incident highlighted the strict expectations of Sensation Black’s audience for hard dance music and reinforced the event’s commitment to maintaining a pure, genre-specific experience.

The popularity of Sensation Black fluctuated in its later years, and in 2009, the event was held only in Belgium and not in the Netherlands. Over time, ID\&T decided to focus on other international ventures, leading to the eventual discontinuation of Sensation Black as a standalone event. While Sensation White continued to evolve and expand internationally, Sensation Black’s legacy remains as one of the defining events for fans of hard dance music, celebrated for its unique contribution to the genre and its influence on similar festivals across Europe.

Sensation White, now simply known as Sensation, is one of the most iconic electronic music events in the world. It was founded by ID\&T, a Dutch events company, and held its first edition at the Amsterdam ArenA in 2000. The event quickly became known for its unique and strict all-white dress code, a concept introduced as a tribute to one of the co-founders, Miles Stutterheim, who tragically passed away in a car accident. His brother Duncan Stutterheim, who co-founded ID\&T, asked attendees of the first Sensation to wear white in honor of Miles. The visual impact of the all-white crowd was so striking that it became a tradition for all future editions of the event.

 

The Sensation brand was initially split into two distinct experiences: Sensation White and Sensation Black. While Sensation White focused on uplifting trance, house, and progressive music, Sensation Black catered to harder styles like hardstyle and hardcore. The duality allowed the event to attract diverse audiences and establish itself as a leader in electronic music experiences. Sensation White’s slogan, "Be part of the night, dress in white," became emblematic of the event’s ethos of unity and purity.

 

Over the years, Sensation White expanded globally, with events held in more than 35 countries, including Germany, Australia, Brazil, Russia, and South Korea. Each event featured elaborate stage designs, massive pyrotechnics, synchronized light shows, and some of the biggest names in the electronic music scene, such as Armin van Buuren, Ferry Corsten, and Carl Cox. Sensation White was also known for its iconic anthems, produced by artists like Rank 1, Tiësto, and Armin van Buuren, which became the musical identity for each edition.

 

Sensation's influence wasn’t limited to Europe; its successful expansion into other continents, including massive productions in places like Melbourne and St. Petersburg, demonstrated its global appeal. The event also became known for its thematic storytelling, with editions such as "The Ocean of White," "The Source of Light," and "Innerspace" pushing the boundaries of what a dance music event could be.

 

The final edition of Sensation in its home base of Amsterdam took place in 2017, titled "The Final." This event marked the end of an era for the Dutch edition, drawing over 50,000 attendees. Despite ending its Amsterdam shows, Sensation continued its global journey, with plans for future events in cities like Dubai and Jakarta.

 

The legacy of Sensation White lies in its ability to create a sense of unity and connection through music, fashion, and extraordinary production. It played a pivotal role in shaping the electronic music festival landscape in the early 2000s and continues to inspire and influence the global festival scene today

Ultra Music Festival (UMF) was founded in 1999 by Russell Faibisch and Alex Omes, inspired by the burgeoning electronic dance music (EDM) scene and the desire to create a large-scale festival dedicated to showcasing the best in the genre. The first event took place on Miami Beach’s Collins Park and, despite attracting around 7,000 attendees, the festival faced a financial loss in its debut year. However, this initial setback didn't deter the founders. With headliners like Paul van Dyk, Rabbit in the Moon, and Josh Wink, the festival quickly gained recognition within the electronic music community.

By 2001, Ultra had relocated to its iconic home at Bayfront Park in downtown Miami, allowing for greater attendance and more expansive stages. The festival’s growth was swift, with a two-day format introduced in 2004 and further expanding to three days by 2006. During these years, Ultra’s lineup solidified its reputation, with performances from major artists such as Tiësto, Armin van Buuren, Carl Cox, and Paul Oakenfold. In 2005, the partnership with Carl Cox led to the creation of the Carl Cox & Friends arena, a staple that remains to this day.

One of Ultra’s defining moments was in 2012 when the festival expanded globally, launching events in cities like Buenos Aires, Seoul, and Ibiza. This marked the beginning of Ultra Worldwide, extending its influence to every corner of the electronic music world. Today, Ultra festivals are held in over 20 countries, making it one of the largest global music festival brands.

Throughout its history, Ultra has not only grown in size but also adapted to changes in the music industry. In 2010, it introduced live acts, featuring artists outside of EDM, such as The Black Eyed Peas and The Prodigy, diversifying its appeal. The festival has also pushed the boundaries of technology and production, utilizing cutting-edge visuals, immersive stages, and innovative set designs, which have become a hallmark of the event.

Despite its successes, Ultra has faced several challenges. In 2014, overcrowding and security issues, including an incident where a security guard was critically injured, led to the festival being temporarily banned from Bayfront Park. Ultra relocated to Virginia Key in 2019, but after logistical problems and backlash, it returned to Bayfront Park the following year.

In recent years, Ultra’s influence has only grown, with its Miami edition drawing in over 165,000 attendees and generating substantial economic impact for the city. Even during the COVID-19 pandemic, Ultra adapted by hosting virtual editions, ensuring that the festival’s reach continued globally. Today, Ultra Music Festival is recognized not only as one of the premier electronic music events but also as a cultural phenomenon that has helped shape the global EDM landscape.

The Winter Music Conference (WMC), established in 1985 by Louis Possenti and Bill Kelly, is one of the longest-standing electronic music conferences in the world. Its original purpose was to create a platform for dance music professionals to connect, share knowledge, and discuss emerging trends in the industry. Over time, WMC evolved into a global gathering, making Miami a key hub for electronic music.

The first WMC was a modest event, held in Fort Lauderdale with only about 80 attendees, mainly consisting of DJs, promoters, radio representatives, and label executives. It was a time when dance music was still a niche genre, far from the commercial powerhouse it would later become. In the early years, the conference provided a crucial networking opportunity for artists and industry insiders to showcase their talent and new music. It was during these formative years that some of the biggest names in dance music, like Carl Cox and Paul Oakenfold, first connected with the industry at large.

One of the major components of WMC is the International Dance Music Awards (IDMAs), which have been a central part of the event since its inception. The IDMAs honor excellence in categories such as best DJ, best track, and best remix, and have become one of the most prestigious awards in the electronic music industry, garnering millions of votes worldwide.

By the late 1990s and early 2000s, WMC became the premier destination for electronic music professionals. The rapid growth of dance music, particularly with the advent of trance, house, and later, progressive and EDM, coincided with WMC’s expansion. Miami’s nightlife and club scene, anchored by venues such as Space, provided the perfect backdrop for this transformation. The conference also played a significant role in the rise of Ultra Music Festival, which began in 1999 and eventually grew to become one of the largest electronic music festivals in the world.

In the early 2010s, WMC faced new challenges. One of the biggest came in 2011, when the conference and Ultra Music Festival, which had always been linked, took place on separate weekends. This created tension among event organizers, artists, and attendees. Many artists were forced to choose between playing at WMC or Ultra, which in turn led to the conference losing some of its traditional prominence. WMC also reduced its length in 2015 from ten days to five, marking a shift in the structure of the event.

In 2018, Ultra Music Festival officially acquired WMC, integrating the two events more closely once again. This acquisition helped revitalize the conference, with a new focus on music production, technology, and innovation in the digital era. WMC became a critical platform for discussing the future of electronic music, incorporating panels, workshops, and keynotes from major industry figures, while continuing to feature performances from both established and emerging artists.

Though WMC faced disruption during the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, the conference returned with renewed energy and has remained an essential event for professionals in the electronic music world. Today, WMC remains one of the leading conferences for DJs, producers, promoters, and fans, offering a unique combination of education, performance, and networking opportunities, helping shape the future of the electronic music industry. Its legacy is deeply tied to the rise of electronic music and its global influence on modern club culture.

Ferry Corsten, born on December 4, 1973, in Rotterdam, Netherlands, is one of the most influential figures in trance music. He began DJing at the age of 15, inspired by Dutch radio DJs like Ferry Maat and Ben Liebrand. Early in his career, Corsten experimented with different aliases, producing hardcore and gabber tracks before transitioning into trance and house music. His first significant breakthrough came in 1996 under the alias Moonman with the track Don’t Be Afraid, which charted in the UK and signaled his arrival on the European dance scene.

In 1999, Corsten released one of his most iconic tracks, Out of the Blue, under his System F alias. This track was a game-changer for trance, achieving international success and landing in the UK Top 20. Around the same time, Corsten also formed Gouryella with fellow Dutch DJ Tiësto, releasing hits like Gouryella and Walhalla, which became anthems in the trance world. He later continued Gouryella as a solo project after Tiësto's departure, releasing Ligaya in 2002.

Corsten's remixing talents became highly sought after, with notable works like William Orbit's Barber’s Adagio for Strings and Silence by Delerium featuring Sarah McLachlan. He also launched Vandit Records and became known for his Trance Nation compilations with Ministry of Sound.

Throughout the 2000s, Corsten produced more trance hits, including collaborations like Exhale with Armin van Buuren and Fire featuring Simon Le Bon of Duran Duran. His 2003 album Right of Way and the follow-up, L.E.F. in 2006, displayed his evolving style, blending electro and house elements with trance.

In 2015, Corsten revived Gouryella with the release of Anahera, which became one of the biggest trance tracks of the year. He continued to push boundaries with the What The F tour, a live performance concept that combined all his major aliases like System F and Gouryella into one immersive show.

Today, Corsten remains one of the most respected names in the electronic music scene, continuously releasing music under his various aliases, performing worldwide, and contributing to the evolution of trance and electronic music as a whole.

Paul van Dyk, born Matthias Paul on December 16, 1971, in Eisenhüttenstadt, East Germany, is a seminal figure in the development of trance music. Growing up in East Berlin, his early exposure to Western radio, such as the broadcasts of electronic and synth-pop music, helped shape his sound. After the fall of the Berlin Wall, Paul began DJing in clubs like Tresor and E-Werk, where he honed his distinctive trance style. His breakthrough came in 1993 with the release of his remix of Humate’s "Love Stimulation," which became a cornerstone of early trance music.

In 1994, he released his debut album 45 RPM, featuring one of his most iconic tracks, "For an Angel," a song that would later be recognized as a defining anthem of trance music. His second album, Seven Ways 1996, solidified his presence in the global electronic music scene, particularly in the UK, where singles like "Forbidden Fruit" and "Beautiful Place" became dancefloor hits.

By the late 90s, Paul van Dyk was a regular at major clubs and festivals around the world, including residencies at iconic venues like Gatecrasher in the UK. In 2000, he released Out There and Back, featuring hits like "Tell Me Why The Riddle" and "We Are Alive," which expanded his reach into more mainstream electronic music.

During the early 2000s, Paul van Dyk founded Vandit Records, and his album Reflections 2003 earned him a Grammy nomination for Best Electronic Album, a first for a DJ. Tracks like "Nothing But You" became massive hits, and he followed this success with his politically charged mix album series The Politics of Dancing.

Tragedy struck in 2016 when Paul suffered a near-fatal fall during a performance at A State of Trance festival, which caused severe injuries and a long recovery. Despite this setback, he returned to producing and performing, releasing albums like From Then On 2017 and Music Rescues Me 2018.

Van Dyk remains one of the most influential figures in electronic music, pioneering the trance sound that has shaped the genre for decades and continuing to perform at major festivals and clubs around the world.

Tijs Michiel Verwest, known as Tiësto, was born on January 17, 1969, in Breda, Netherlands. He began DJing at the age of 14 and built his early reputation by playing at small local clubs, particularly The Spock in Breda. Here, he honed his style, initially mixing new beat and acid house, which laid the foundation for his future trance sound.

In the early 90s, Tiësto released hardcore and gabber tracks under aliases like Da Joker and DJ Limited. His big break came in 1994 when he signed to Basic Beat Recordings. During this period, he teamed up with Arny Bink, and in 1997, they founded Black Hole Recordings, the label that would propel him to global fame. Before his massive success with the Magik series, Tiësto curated the Forbidden Paradise compilations. These early mixes, particularly Forbidden Paradise 3 The Quest for Atlantis 1995 and Forbidden Paradise 6 Valley of Fire 1996, showcased his evolving trance style and helped build his reputation in the European trance community.

The launch of his Magik series in 1997, under Black Hole Recordings, marked a turning point. The series established him as a key figure in the trance scene, and by 1999, Tiësto had partnered with fellow Dutch DJ Ferry Corsten to create Gouryella. Their singles Gouryella and Walhalla became iconic trance tracks, solidifying Tiësto’s status in the electronic music world. Around this time, Tiësto also debuted his In Search of Sunrise series, starting with the first edition in 1999, further establishing his melodic and uplifting trance sound.

In 2000, Tiësto reached new heights with his release of Summerbreeze, a compilation that included his now-legendary remix of Delerium’s Silence featuring Sarah McLachlan. The remix catapulted him into mainstream success, especially in the United States. This momentum carried forward with his debut studio album, In My Memory 2001, which featured some of his most iconic tracks, including Suburban Train, Lethal Industry, and Flight 643. These tracks became staples of his live sets and helped solidify his global appeal.

Tiësto's next major milestone was his second studio album, Just Be 2004, which included hits like Traffic, Love Comes Again, and the title track Just Be. These tracks became anthems in the trance world and showcased Tiësto's ability to craft dancefloor hits. During this period, Tiësto also held residencies at clubs like Amnesia in Ibiza, a key location for his career during the early 2000s.

In 2004, Tiësto became the first DJ to perform at the Olympic Games, playing live at the opening ceremony in Athens. His performance marked a historic moment, both for Tiësto and the broader electronic music scene, as he brought electronic music to a global audience.

In the mid-2000s, Tiësto began exploring new sounds, moving from trance towards progressive house and other genres. His 2007 album Elements of Life marked a shift, reaching number one on the Billboard Top Electronic Albums chart. By this time, Tiësto had become one of the biggest names in electronic music, headlining festivals such as Ultra Music Festival and Tomorrowland.

In 2009, Tiësto sold his stake in Black Hole Recordings and founded Musical Freedom, a label that allowed him to explore more commercial electronic music sounds. His album Kaleidoscope 2009 further pushed him away from his trance roots and towards a more accessible pop-electronic fusion. Collaborations with mainstream artists like Nelly Furtado on Who Wants to Be Alone and the electro-house hit Feel It In My Bones with Tegan and Sara demonstrated his evolving sound.

In recent years, Tiësto has continued to dominate the electronic music scene, with albums like A Town Called Paradise 2014 and The London Sessions 2020. He also won a Grammy in 2015 for Best Remixed Recording with his remix of John Legend's All of Me. Despite moving away from his trance origins, his early works like Forbidden Paradise, Magik, and In Search of Sunrise remain classics that defined an era of trance music and solidified Tiësto’s legacy as one of the greatest electronic music pioneers.

Between 2020 and 2024, Tiësto continued to release chart-topping singles such as The Business 2020, Don't Be Shy 2021 featuring Karol G, and 10 35 2022. These tracks showcased his adaptability, blending electronic music with pop elements, and solidifying his influence across multiple generations of electronic music fans.

Phonk is a subgenre of hip-hop that originated in the Southern United States in the early 2010s. It draws heavy influence from 1990s Memphis rap, characterized by its lo-fi production, gritty sound, and use of samples from older rap records, funk, jazz, and soul. The genre often features chopped and screwed vocal samples, eerie melodies, and heavy basslines, creating a dark, atmospheric sound that resonates with a sense of nostalgia for the raw, underground hip-hop of the 1990s.

The roots of phonk can be traced back to the underground hip-hop scene of Memphis, Tennessee, where artists like DJ Screw, Three 6 Mafia, and Tommy Wright III produced music with dark, minimalist beats and lyrics focused on street life, crime, and the occult. Phonk artists in the 2010s began to incorporate these elements into their music, using modern production techniques to create a blend of old-school Southern rap and contemporary sounds.

Phonk's rise in popularity was significantly boosted by the internet, particularly through platforms like SoundCloud and YouTube, where producers and artists could share their work with a global audience. Artists like SpaceGhostPurrp, DJ Smokey, and $uicideboy$ played a key role in popularizing the genre, blending the gritty aesthetic of 90s Memphis rap with elements of trap, lo-fi, and cloud rap.

The genre is often associated with a DIY ethic, with many phonk producers using software like FL Studio to create their tracks, and releasing music independently on digital platforms. Its lo-fi, sample-heavy style and use of analog sounds contrast with the polished production found in mainstream hip-hop, giving phonk a raw, unfiltered edge.

Phonk continues to evolve, with modern artists incorporating more diverse influences and production techniques while maintaining the genre's distinctive dark and atmospheric vibe. Its underground ethos and nostalgic sound have helped it maintain a dedicated fan base, particularly among listeners who appreciate the genre's homage to the early days of Southern hip-hop.

Essential tracks:

- "Mystikal Maze" by DJ Smokey: A track that encapsulates the dark, sample-heavy production and eerie atmosphere typical of phonk music.
- "Pheel tha Phonk" by Soudiere: Known for its lo-fi beats and chopped vocal samples, this track highlights the genre's blend of old-school and modern hip-hop elements.
- "Southside Suicide" by $uicideboy$ & Pouya: A collaboration that combines the gritty, raw energy of phonk with trap influences, showcasing the genre's versatility.

Baile funk, also known as funk carioca, is a genre of music that originated in the favelas of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, in the early 1990s. It emerged as a local response to the influence of American hip-hop and electronic music, blending these influences with traditional Brazilian rhythms and cultural elements.

The genre's name, "baile funk," reflects its roots in the "bailes," or parties, that were common in Rio's favelas. Funk carioca is characterized by its energetic beats, catchy hooks, and rhythmic vocal delivery. It often features a mix of rap and singing, with lyrics addressing a range of social issues, from party life to struggles with poverty.

The early sound of baile funk was heavily influenced by Miami bass and Miami bass-influenced hip-hop, with its use of heavy bass lines and rhythmic beats. Pioneering producers such as DJ Marlboro and the group Bonde do Tigrão were instrumental in shaping the genre's distinctive sound, incorporating elements of samba, pagode, and other Brazilian music styles into their tracks.

Throughout the 2000s, baile funk gained wider recognition beyond Brazil, thanks in part to the internet and international collaborations. Artists like Diplo and M.I.A. began to incorporate baile funk elements into their music, helping to introduce the genre to a global audience.
